nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater streams and forest peat swamps with dense leaf litter, submerged roots, and riparian vegetation; highly stained, extremely soft and acidic water with near-zero hardness

decorPreferences: dense planting with fine-leaved or floating plants (Java moss, Microsorum, Salvinia)
leaf litter (catappa/Indian almond leaves) for tannins and shelter
driftwood and twisted roots for cover and tannin release
dim lighting or shaded areas via floating plants
dark substrate to enhance coloration and reduce stress
sponge filter or gentle flow only — strong current stresses this species

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on tiny invertebrates, zooplankton, and small insects. In captivity accepts high-quality micro-pellets and fine flake, but thrives with regular live or frozen micro-foods: baby brine shrimp, Daphnia, micro worms, cyclops, and frozen rotifers. Small particle size is essential — standard flake foods may be too large for the tiny mouth. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: small peaceful Betta species (e.g., B. imbellis)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina — adult shrimp safe; shrimp-safe species)
avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them (most standard community fish)
boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete them for food
fish requiring hard alkaline water (incompatible chemistry)
baby shrimp (fry may be predated by the rasboras themselves)

Photographer: Andrewbogott. Uploaded 2014-10-21. 1197×722 px, 942 KB. Shows female specimen.

summary: A tiny (~2 cm) blackwater micro-rasbora endemic to Borneo's peat swamps. Distinguished from the similar Chili Rasbora (B. brigittae) by its bronzy-copper base coloration and thicker dark midline stripe. Peaceful and shrimp-safe; best kept in soft, acidic, tannin-rich nano setups in groups of 8 or more.
careNotes:
The Phoenix Rasbora (Boraras merah) is one of the smallest cyprinids in the aquarium hobby, reaching only ~2 cm. It is endemic to the Jelai Bila river basin of Central Kalimantan, Indonesia, where it inhabits heavily shaded, tannin-stained peat swamp streams with extremely soft (near-zero hardness), acidic (pH 4–6.5) water. Replicating these blackwater conditions is key to long-term success: use RO or very soft source water, add Indian almond leaves or alder cones for tannins, and include driftwood. Hardness and alkalinity should be kept very low (gH 0–5, kH 0–2); fish kept in even moderately hard water may show faded coloration and shortened lifespan. A 40–60 L well-planted nano aquarium with a sponge filter (minimal current) is ideal for a colony of 8–12.

This species is frequently mislabeled as the Chili Rasbora (B. brigittae) in the trade. B. merah has a bronzy/coppery body base and a thicker, more diffuse dark lateral stripe, while B. brigittae is a brighter, more saturated red with a thinner, sharper stripe. Both require similar care, but knowing which species you have aids accurate record-keeping.

Phoenix Rasboras are shrimp-safe with adult dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina), making them popular for planted shrimp tanks. However, newly born shrimp shrimplets may occasionally be predated. Keep only with tankmates of similar small size and comparable water parameter requirements — most standard tropical community fish prefer harder and more alkaline water than this species tolerates. Dim lighting with ample shade (floating plants, dense planting) is strongly recommended; the fish displays brightest coloration and lowest stress under subdued light. Keep in groups of at least 8; isolated individuals become shy and may refuse food.
breedingNotes: Egg-scattering, continuous spawner with no parental care — adults will eat their own eggs and fry if given the opportunity. Condition breeders with live and frozen micro-foods. Use a small breeding tank (10–20 L) with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.5, gH 0–2) and fine-leaved spawning mops or Java moss. Eggs are tiny and adhesive; they hatch in approximately 48 hours. Remove adults after spawning, or provide very dense plant cover. Fry require infusoria or commercial micro-fry foods for the first week before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms. Breeding is achievable in well-maintained blackwater setups but is rated moderate difficulty due to the tiny fry size and their sensitivity to water quality.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Boraras merah
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (15–20 mm SL), temperature (20–28°C), pH (4.0–6.5), hardness (18–90 ppm / approx. gH 1–5), native range (Jelai Bila river basin, Central Kalimantan, Indonesia), biotope (blackwater peat swamp), diet (micropredator), minimum tank base (45×30 cm), schooling group size (8–10 minimum), breeding method and difficulty. Used as primary authority for care parameters.

additional.name: FishBase — Boraras merah (ID 22603)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (2.0 cm SL), native range (western and southern Borneo), taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes, Rasborinae), trophic level 3.1 (consistent with invertivore/micropredator), IUCN status Data Deficient (2019). FishBase lists family as Danionidae (formerly placed in Cyprinidae — reflecting modern molecular phylogeny; Danionidae is the currently accepted family for Boraras).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Chili Rasbora Care Guide
additional.notes: Covers B. brigittae (closest relative / trade substitute for B. merah); confirms shared care requirements: temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0 (trade/pragmatic range), group size 8–12 minimum, diet of frozen rotifers, cyclops, baby brine shrimp, micro worms. Minimum tank noted as 11 L (3 gal) for B. brigittae; 40 L chosen for B. merah per SeriouslyFish 45×30 cm base and to allow adequate group size with filtration.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; continuous spawner with no parental care. Requires very soft acidic water (pH 5–6.5, gH 0–2), fine-leaved plants or spawning mops, and separation of adults post-spawn. Fry are tiny and require infusoria initially. Achievable in dedicated setups but challenging due to fry fragility.
